coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation on thermal management of lithium-ion battery pack for formula student electric vehicle using air-cooling system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wankhede, Sagar
Pingale, Ajay D.
Kale, Atharva
coredata.dc:description: The increasing adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) has driven extensive research and development efforts to optimize the performance and safety of their energy-storage systems, particularly lithium-ion battery packs (LIBPs). Elevated temperatures in EV batteries primarily result from thermal instability during various operating, traveling, and charging conditions. In formula student electric vehicle (FSEV) competitions, where efficiency and reliability are critical, effective cooling of the battery pack (BP) is essential. This study analyzed the cooling performance of an air-cooled thermal management system using relevant system parameters and precise thermal modeling through CFD simulations. Various cooling parameters, such as coolant flow rate, fan speed, and cooling channel geometry, were systematically adjusted to evaluate their effects on BP temperature distribution, thermal equilibrium, and overall performance. Key metrics, including maximum temperature and temperature distribution within the battery module, were used to compare simulation results and optimize outcomes for future applications. Experiments validated the simulations of the optimal solution. The results of this investigation provide valuable insights for designing and improving active cooling systems for LIBPs in FSEVs. The average variance of the obtained temperature data was 4.256% based on simulation results. At an air velocity of 17 m·s−1, the BP temperature remained within the ideal range of 30–40 °C. Enhanced cooling strategies can improve the thermal stability of BPs, extend their lifespan, and reduce the risk of thermal runaway.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Air-cooling system
Heat transfer
Thermal performance

Cell selection as per the market survey
Influence of the air velocity on battery temperature
CFD analysis of duct and BP
Experimental testing of the battery modules

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Drive cycle input (custom drive cycle according to formula student race car).
Case I: rise in cell module temperature at v = 2 m·s− 1.
Case II: rise in cell module temperature at v = 5 m·s−¹.
Case III: temperature rise of cell module at v = 10 m·s−¹.
Case IV: rise in cell module temperature at v = 15 m·s−¹.
Case V: rise in cell module temperature at v = 17 m·s−¹.
Schematic representation of cross flow (a) and serpentine flow (b) through the battery pack.
Photos of assembled module (a) and battery pack (b).
Experimental test setup for a single battery pack module (enlarged section depicts the sensor mounting).

Life expectancy (continuous operation at 40 °C with 15% −65% RH) (h)

head.ce:title: Experimental investigation on thermal management of lithium-ion battery pack for formula student electric vehicle using air-cooling system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Pimpri Chinchwad College of Engineering, Pune, Maharashtra, 411044, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Pimpri Chinchwad College of Engineering, Pune, 411044, Maharashtra, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) has driven extensive research and development efforts to optimize the performance and safety of their energy-storage systems, particularly lithium-ion battery packs (LIBPs). Elevated temperatures in EV batteries primarily result from thermal instability during various operating, traveling, and charging conditions. In formula student electric vehicle (FSEV) competitions, where efficiency and reliability are critical, effective cooling of the battery pack (BP) is essential. This study analyzed the cooling performance of an air-cooled thermal management system using relevant system parameters and precise thermal modeling through CFD simulations. Various cooling parameters, such as coolant flow rate, fan speed, and cooling channel geometry, were systematically adjusted to evaluate their effects on BP temperature distribution, thermal equilibrium, and overall performance. Key metrics, including maximum temperature and temperature distribution within the battery module, were used to compare simulation results and optimize outcomes for future applications. Experiments validated the simulations of the optimal solution. The results of this investigation provide valuable insights for designing and improving active cooling systems for LIBPs in FSEVs. The average variance of the obtained temperature data was 4.256% based on simulation results. At an air velocity of 17 m·s−1, the BP temperature remained within the ideal range of 30–40 °C. Enhanced cooling strategies can improve the thermal stability of BPs, extend their lifespan, and reduce the risk of thermal runaway.

ce:section.ce:para: Electric vehicles (EVs) are the best option for reducing the environmental problems and pollution caused by traditional internal combustion engine automobiles [1,2]. An essential part of an EV is the battery [3–6]. In electric and hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), a central challenge pertains to the internal heat liberation observed during the charge-discharge cycles of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). To address this concern and ensure the durability and optimal functioning of battery packs (BPs), it is imperative to incorporate effective Battery Thermal Management Systems (BTMS) [7]. In the past two decades, the automotive industry has witnessed notable advances in BTMS technologies aimed at ameliorating the inherent thermal challenges of LIBs. The sustained performance and longevity of BPs are directly proportional to their ability to maintain an optimal temperature range throughout charge-discharge cycles.

Pioneering research has yielded diverse thermal management strategies including air cooling [8–10], liquid cooling [11–13], phase-change material cooling [14–16], and heat pipe cooling [17,18]. Air-cooling methodologies include both forced and natural convection processes, each with distinct advantages and limitations. By contrast, liquid cooling modalities comprise indirect and direct cooling methods, providing various design and implementation possibilities [19]. Recognized as a form of thermal storage technology [20–24], phase change material (PCM) has emerged as a viable solution for the efficient regulation of BP temperatures.

Among the aforementioned technologies, air and liquid cooling are most frequently used to meet the demands of the automobile industry. Because of its straightforward design, lower manufacturing costs, lighter weight, simpler maintenance requirements, and improved system reliability, this effort was planned to concentrate on air-cooling technology [25]. However, to achieve the same cooling effectiveness, a substantially higher volumetric flow rate is required because air has a much lower heat capacity than liquids. The heat produced by the battery cell is dissipated by air in the air-cooled BTMS. There are two categories of cooling techniques: passive and active [26]. Low-density batteries often utilize the former, which is passive and only uses ambient conditions, whereas high-density batteries typically use the latter, which is active and employs fans or blowers to increase convection. Because of the improved performance of BTMSs, forced convection is widely used in the automotive industry. According to previous reports, forced convection can be classified into passive and active air convections [27].

Compared with other cooling methods, air cooling is less costly, and it is simple to choose the right system. Consequently, air cooling is one of the most popular approaches for battery temperature control. Most BPs for which air-based BTMSs have been proposed in the literature have modest cooling requirements. Axial ventilation increases the space used by the BP and improves the power density of EVs fitted with cylindrical batteries [28]. To significantly enhance the capacity of air-cooled BTMS’s for heat dissipation, some researchers have explored altering the BP construction [29]. Yang et al. [30] examined the link between the temperature rise of the battery and its spacing while maintaining a constant airflow rate, as well as the heat dispersion efficiency of a 18,650 battery under different layouts. Li et al. [31] examined the impact of cell spacing on the thermal efficiency and presented findings on the optimization of design parameters using an optimization technique. According to the findings, the highest temperature gradient and standard deviation have significantly improved. By altering the air inlet and outlet sites, Xu and He [32] investigated the effect of the type of airflow duct on the ability of the battery module to disperse heat. According to these findings, reducing the airflow channel decreased the temperature of the battery module. Chen et al. [33] investigated the impact of various air inlet and outlet positions on the cooling efficiency. To further enhance the cooling capacity, an optimization approach for the locations of the air inflow and outflow was suggested based on the ideal plan. The maximum battery temperature difference after optimization was decreased by 7.7 K and 1.7 K, respectively, when compared to the Z-shaped BTMS and symmetrical systems. The volume of the BTMS did not decrease even though the aforementioned research enhanced the ability of the battery module to dissipate heat.

According to the findings presented in a study by E et al. [34], the optimal cooling efficacy was observed when the air inlet and outlet points were situated on opposite sides, complemented by the inclusion of a baffle plate. The introduction of the baffle plate modified the airflow direction, thereby contributing to augmented cooling. In a study conducted by Zhang et al. [35], the influence of incorporating spoilers on the side of the air intake was investigated to assess its impact on the battery’s heat dissipation capabilities. This study further explored the correlation between the number and positioning of spoilers and the resultant temperature levels within a BTMS. Their findings indicated that an optimal reduction of 1.8 K in the maximum temperature was achieved when the quantity and placement of spoilers were appropriately configured. In addition, introducing spoilers to the initial cooling channel resulted in a notable reduction of 3.39 K in the maximum temperature. The Formula Student Electric Vehicle (FSEV) Issue Statement includes several significant issues. The first and most important limitation is the amount of space that can be used to fit the BP and functional battery thermal management system. The requirement to control the weight of the BP exacerbates the space restrictions. Weight gain may lead to increased energy consumption during driving, which reduces the vehicle performance and range. Owing to spatial constraints, there is a trade-off between vehicle performance and battery capacity. The difficulty lies in achieving high vehicle performance without sacrificing limited battery capacity. This frequently implies a higher battery discharge rate, which increases heat generation. Consequently, an efficient BTMS is required to manage and dissipate the heat. Capacity to manage high-power requirements during braking and acceleration in race situations, where rapid charging and discharging cycles can produce a large amount of heat.

Efficiency, safety, and innovative thermal management are the main criteria used in Formula Bharat to evaluate the viability of EVs. In addition to improving the performance, efficient battery heat management guarantees both driver safety and battery longevity during competition. A recurring pattern was identified through a thorough review of the literature and evaluation of the state of battery thermal management technologies used in EVs. Forced-air cooling is the primary technique of choice for fabricating formula student electric vehicles (FSEVs). Although there have been some attempts to combine forced air cooling with heat pipes, liquid cooling systems, or PCM cooling systems, this method is not always effective as BP sizes grow and charge-discharge rates rise. Interestingly, few studies have been conducted on battery temperature management using various airflow patterns. However, using different flow patterns seems promising because they can assist in overcoming the FSEV size constraints while providing high-temperature sustainability. Saving money is another benefit of using air as the cooling medium.

To close this gap, this study evaluated whether forced air cooling with various flow patterns and velocities is appropriate for FSEV thermal control. This study aims to improve battery thermal management in the EV sector by identifying the optimal cooling effect for FSEV BP applications.

Among a variety of electronic products, EVs, and renewable energy storage systems, LIBs are popular rechargeable batteries. They offer several benefits compared to other battery types, such as nickel-cadmium and lead-acid batteries [7]. LIBs are unique owing to their high energy density, lightweight construction, efficiency, lack of memory, and long cycle life. Many contemporary applications, particularly portable electronics and electric cars, are the preferred choices.
LIBs are produced in three primary formats [13]:

The cylindrical cell consists of cathode, anode, electrode, and separator layers that form its electrochemical structure. These layers are assembled into a “jelly roll” configuration and wound around a steel core within a cylindrical housing, resulting in a compact cell design.

The prismatic cell has a construction similar to cylindrical cells but features flat, rectangular housing instead of a cylindrical shape, which reduces the cell’s overall thickness. The housing is typically made from steel or aluminum cans for added durability.

The pouch cell is enclosed in a flexible plastic-aluminum casing, often in a thin-foil form. Inside the casing, terminal tabs are welded to the current collectors after puncturing the seals. These tabs serve to encapsulate the cells and provide external connection points.

LIBs generate heat when charged and discharged, and high temperatures reduce their performance. Throughout the entire BP, the temperature of the cells must be tightly maintained within the optimal running temperature range of 15 °C to 45 °C [7,9]. Rapid cell deterioration and reduced battery life can be caused by thermal problems such as overheating and uneven temperature distribution. Under extreme conditions, thermal runaway (TRA) may occur when the temperature of cells surpasses a critical threshold, initiating exothermic reactions within functional cells [36]. This initiation typically occurs around 90 °C, leading to the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) and the release of additional heat, resulting in uncontrolled heat generation that can lead to fires or explosions.

Temperature management has therefore emerged as the primary challenge in LIB development. Temperature-related issues adversely affect the performance, lifespan, and safety of batteries. To address these concerns, systems have been developed to ensure consistent temperature distribution, regulate the BP within the optimal range of 15 °C to 35 °C for maximum performance and longevity, and mitigate the risk of uncontrolled temperature increases or TRA. Effective thermal management is crucial for overcoming these challenges. Additionally, voltage protection is an essential function of battery management systems. Like traditional internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles, EVs and hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) require cooling systems for their BPs. The efficiency and cost of BP thermal management systems depend on the chosen cooling technology.

Over the past two decades, numerous BTMSs have been developed to precisely regulate the optimal temperature range within BPs.
i.
Air-cooled strategy
ii.
Liquid-cooled strategy
iii.
PCM-cooled strategy
iv.
Heat pipe-cooled strategy

Among the available technologies, air cooling systems and liquid cooling systems are the most commonly used to meet the automobile industry’s requirements. This study focused on air-cooling technology due to its straightforward design, lower manufacturing cost, lighter weight, simpler maintenance, and improved system reliability. However, achieving similar cooling effectiveness with air requires a significantly higher volumetric flow rate because air has a much lower heat capacity than liquids.

A review of the literature revealed that most studies have focused on cylindrical cells using the air-cooling method, primarily at low discharge rates. This study, however, emphasizes the cooling of pouch cells at high discharge rates. Heat dissipation was estimated using MATLAB. Appropriate MATLAB and SIMULINK models were developed, and the results were compared with actual values. In this research, various parameters were analyzed for cooling the BP in a Formula Bharat car, and the results demonstrate that temperatures can be maintained within the optimal range of 40 °C to 50 °C.

This paper’s problem formulation, major contribution, primary motivation, implications, and findings may be summarized as follows:
•
To satisfy the voltage and current requirements, the BP configuration was determined as 112s2p.
•
To simulate the BP, a single module was considered using ANSYS fluent.
•
To explore the different flow patterns of air through the BP.
•
The impact of air velocity was investigated to further enhance the cooling effect.
•
To compare simulation results with experimental results.

Section 2 describes the methodology to be followed, and cell selection, cell chemistry, comparison of cells based on electrical and physical parameters, market survey, design of BP, and fan selection are defined in Section 3. Section 4 presents the results and discussion of different parameters, assumptions, experimentation, and validation. Section 5 discusses the limitations of this study. Finally, Section 6 presents the conclusions.

Fig. 1 presents a flowchart summarizing the study’s methodology. This flowchart outlines the sequential phases from problem definition to testing and outcome validation, providing a clear representation of the project’s approach. The process begins with precise problem identification and goal setting. A comprehensive literature review is then conducted to gather current information and identify gaps. The components are designed and tested to ensure compliance with the required specifications. If the design fails to meet the specifications, the process is repeated. Once the components meet the requirements, a market survey is conducted to identify similar products and assist with procurement decisions. After the design phase, components are manufactured, and the necessary resources are procured. Performance is evaluated under real-world conditions through experimentation, which leads to the final stage of testing and validation. This process ensures the project meets its objectives by emphasizing iterative improvements, quality control, and methodological refinement at every stage.

The BP was designed to achieve several objectives: building a BP capable of meeting the demands of the selected motor (EMRAX 208), providing sufficient capacity for a 22-kilometer endurance run, and complying with prescribed rules and general safety practices, including an effective cooling system.

While the weight and size of the cells are critical for making the BP compact and lightweight, additional factors were also considered. These include energy density, nominal voltage, cell capacity, operating temperature, power density, continuous discharge rate, pulse discharge rate, and availability. Designing a BP requires careful consideration of these parameters to ensure optimal performance. For this BP, the nominal voltage was set to 407 V, with a peak voltage not exceeding 462 V. The peak current was specified at 200 A, and the BP’s capacity was 16 Ah, resulting in a total energy storage of 7.7 kWh. Determining the required operating voltage is the primary factor in deciding the number of cells to connect in series. Configuring cells in parallel focuses on two critical factors: the energy needed to complete one endurance cycle and the current required for the motor to perform at peak capacity. This ensures the BP meets both energy and current demands.

There are four types of lithium-ion chemistries [37] that could be used in our application:
i.
Lithium cobalt oxide (LCO)
ii.
Lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC)
iii.
Lithium nickel cobalt aluminium oxide (NCA)
iv.
Lithium iron phosphate (LFP)

Lithium manganese oxide was excluded due to its low energy density, and lithium titanate was not selected because of its low voltage range. Table 1 [21,37,38] compares battery chemistries based on specific power, specific energy, safety, life span, and cost.
Based on the comparison, the LCO chemistry was selected for the BP design according to the requirements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell selection based on physical and electrical parameters
ce:section.ce:para: Following a literature review on cell chemistries, physical parameters were analyzed, as weight and volume are crucial considerations in BP design. A comparison of the three primary cell types is provided in Table 2 [39]. Cylindrical cells, though more robust and economical, have lower power density and are available in smaller capacities, necessitating more cells to meet capacity requirements. Prismatic cells, typically available in very high capacities, are unsuitable for EV applications. Pouch cells, by contrast, offer high energy storage density, reduced space requirements compared to cylindrical cells, and significant power per kilogram of energy storage. Based on these advantages, pouch cells were chosen for the BP due to their high energy density and low internal resistance. While cylindrical cells are cheaper and more durable, their assembly is more complex, and reconfiguration is not feasible once the pack is completed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell selection as per the market survey
ce:section.ce:para: Key factors considered for selecting the pouch cell included capacity, energy density, discharge rate, and weight. A market survey was conducted to identify the most suitable cell type. Table 3 [40–43] presents a comparison of the pouch cells.

After a market survey, it was determined that the LCO cell manufactured by Melasta had higher energy density and lower internal resistance compared to other cells. Therefore, the Melasta cell was chosen for the final design due to its superior energy density and low resistance. A photograph of the Melasta cell is shown in Fig. 2 .

The cell selection was based on several parameters, including energy requirements, current demands, and internal resistance. To meet the energy requirement of 7.7 kWh at a system voltage of 462 V, an approximate current capacity of 16.8 Ah was needed. The motor specifications required a maximum current of 200 A and a nominal current of 90 A. Consequently, the cells were selected to meet these criteria. The decision to use an 8.4 Ah cell instead of a 16 Ah cell was based on the observation that internal resistance remained relatively constant regardless of capacity. By connecting two lower-capacity cells in parallel, the overall resistance decreased compared to using a single higher-capacity cell, thereby reducing losses in the BP. As a result, the SLPBA347145 cell manufactured by Shenzhen Melasta was chosen for the BP design.

To meet the voltage and current requirements, the BP configuration was set to 112S2P. For serviceability and safety, the BP was divided into six segments. The BP layout is shown in Fig. 3 . A total of 224 cells were used to fulfill the energy requirements. To meet the voltage specifications, 112 cells were connected in series. The BP was divided into six segments, each configured as 18S2P or 20S2P. These segments were interconnected in series to form the complete BP and provide the required energy output. Each segment included its own cooling channel with separate fresh-air inlets, enabling simultaneous cooling. The overall dimensions of the BP were 546 mm (width) × 450 mm (length) × 310 mm (height). Since each segment was cooled individually, analysis and simulation were conducted on a single module to evaluate the overall cooling performance of the pack.

A heat generation model was developed using MATLAB software, as shown in Fig. 4 , based on the cell configuration within a single module of the BP. The model accounted for heat generation influenced by factors such as internal resistance and electrical contact resistance within the cells. It was constructed using Simulink and Simscape libraries available in MATLAB. A cell-position model was created to represent the internal resistance of the cells across various temperature and state of charge ranges. This model also included energy losses due to entropy changes. The simulation was extended to cover the entire pack to evaluate heat dissipation within the BP. The model also incorporated heat transfer mechanisms involving air and the tabs connected to bus bars.

For heat generation analysis, the current was run through the pack model, which calculated and outputted the heat generated per cell for the entire pack. The current drive cycle used for the simulation was based on data from an actual FS vehicle operating on an FS track. Data recorded during the previous season served as input for the simulations since driver responses during a performance run were highly consistent. The input included a 1,200-second section of the run, corresponding to an 11 km distance, as shown in Fig. 5 . The plot in Fig. 6 shows the heat generated in a module over the drive cycle, with peaks reaching approximately 800 W. The average heat generated in the module is 174.9 W.

Fig. 7 illustrates the BP or cell temperature without cooling, where the applied heat load causes a maximum temperature rise of up to 90 °C. In contrast, Fig. 8 depicts the temperature graph of the cell module with cooling, demonstrating that the maximum temperature is controlled below 55 °C.

Active cooling technologies improve heat dissipation using an external cooling fan. The fan helps maintain the battery within the optimal temperature range by accelerating heat loss through enhanced fluid flow. Cooling fans were installed at the inlet and outlet sections at the top of the module to direct airflow over the battery for cooling. This forced-air cooling mechanism significantly enhances heat dissipation. After comparing various models, DELTA model No AFB1512HH-AF00 was selected for the experiments.

Table 4 [44,45] presents a technical comparison between the DELTA fan models FFB0412SHN and AFB1512HH-AF00, highlighting differences in input power, speed, maximum airflow, and life expectancy. The AFB1512HH-AF00 model offers an airflow capacity of 6.084 m3·min−1, significantly higher than the FFB0412SHN’s 0.680 m3·min−1, resulting in more effective heat dissipation. Additionally, the AFB1512HH-AF00 has a longer lifespan of 80,000 h and operates at a lower acoustic noise level, making it a well-balanced choice for applications requiring both efficiency and quiet performance. Its durability and superior cooling performance make it particularly suitable for harsh operating environments.
Thermal and CFD simulations were also conducted. The required CAD models for these simulations were prepared using CATIA V5 software and subsequently imported into ANSYS for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the air velocity on battery temperature
ce:section.ce:para: The single-module CAD configuration included 36 cells, arranged as 18 parallel-connected pairs in series, with copper used for connections. Material properties were assigned in ANSYS to ensure accurate simulations. With the heat generation in the BP known, the focus shifted to determining heat removal rates via convective cooling. Iterations were performed to optimize the flow rate and achieve the required heat transfer coefficient, ensuring the battery temperature remained below 55 °C. Simulations were conducted using ANSYS Fluent software, analyzing five cases with air velocities of 2, 5, 10, 15, and 17 m·s−¹ to assess their effects on temperature distribution and cooling efficiency within the battery cell module. The boundary conditions included air velocity, a gauge pressure of zero, and an ambient air temperature of 40 °C.

The thermal profile for a battery cell module at an intake air velocity of 2 m·s−¹ is shown in Fig. 9 , with a maximum temperature of 59.5 °C recorded under this configuration. At an intake air velocity of 5 m·s−¹, Fig. 10 illustrates the temperature distribution, with the highest observed temperature being below 57.18 °C.

Fig. 11 depicts the temperature distribution at an inlet air velocity of 10 m·s−¹, where the highest temperature is lower than 51.99 °C. At an inlet air velocity of 15 m·s−¹, as shown in Fig. 12 , the maximum temperature recorded is below 48.42 °C.

The temperature distribution within the battery cell module at an inlet air velocity of 17 m·s−¹ is shown in Fig. 13 , with the highest recorded temperature being 41.28 °C. Based on these iterations, it was concluded that optimum cooling of the module occurs in Case V, at an inlet air velocity of v = 17 m·s−¹, where the temperature remains within the range of 40–50 °C. Thus, an air inlet velocity of v = 17 m·s−¹ was selected as the optimal value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CFD analysis of duct and BP
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 14 illustrates two cooling flow configurations for a BP: cross-flow and serpentine flow. In cross-flow, air moves horizontally across the module, while in serpentine flow, air is directed through a winding path to enhance cooling efficiency. The CFD analyses for these configurations are presented in Figs. 15 and 16
, respectively. For this study, a cross-flow channel was selected for the BP analysis.

After identifying the most effective design, the BP was manufactured for actual testing and validation. The container was constructed from sheet metal aluminum, with the panels assembled and welded to form a robust structure to hold the BP. Cell holders were 3D-printed using acrylonitrile butadiene styrene due to its fire-resistant properties. Cells were placed in these holders, and busbar connections were established. Thermistors were mounted directly onto the busbars using bolts. Terminal busbars were fitted with RADSOK connectors to facilitate easy connection and disconnection for maintenance.

Each module consisted of 36 cells arranged in an 18s2p configuration (18 packs of 2 cells in parallel). Six such modules were assembled and housed in the battery container, forming a complete 470 V BP. Photographs of the assembled module and BP are shown in Fig. 17 . The BP connections were monitored and protected using an Orion BMS 2. Additional safety measures included a Littelfuse LQCMV100 fuse installed in the positive high-current path and two Gigavac GX11 contactors for disconnecting the battery from external connections.

Certain assumptions must be made when building a BTMS for a Bharat EV to guarantee that the system operates well. These assumptions simplify the design process by defining the critical characteristics affecting the performance and behavior of the battery system.

Here are the common assumptions for a BTMS in Formula Bharat EVs:
•
BPs contain lithium-ion cells, which are the most popular batteries for high-performance EV applications because of their energy density, power output, and cycle characteristics.
•
The recommended working temperature range for battery cells is between 20 °C and 40 °C.
•
During racing, BPs are subjected to high power demands and frequent charging and discharging cycles.
•
Events for Formula Bharat are usually conducted in India, where the weather can change dramatically from season to season. Different external temperature conditions might affect the need for heating or cooling; therefore, the BTMS must consider this.
•
Formula Bharat cars must be lightweight; however, liquid cooling may be necessary for rapid heat drainage because of the high thermal requirements of batteries.
•
A considerable amount of heat is produced by the battery, particularly during high-discharge cycles (such as acceleration and regenerative braking).
•
The Bharat vehicle design formula prioritizes minimizing weight and maximizing space for other crucial components (e.g., aerodynamics, motor, and suspension); hence, there are restricted space and weight limits for the BTMS.
•
TRA, in which a single overheated cell can set off a series of events that result in fire or explosion, should be prevented by a BTMS.
•
The BTMS should consume the least energy relative to the entire battery capacity.
•
The battery may require quick charging, which creates additional heat because of the increased current.
•
The BTMS should maintain an even temperature distribution throughout the BP.
•
Budget-conscious and cost-effective design is essential for the BTMS.
•
In the early phases of design, heat generation and dissipation may be estimated using simplified thermal models; however, in later stages, more intricate simulations or testing may be required.
•
The BTMS design will abide by industry standards for the safety of EV batteries (such as ISO 26,262) and the Formula Bharat competition rules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental testing of the battery modules
ce:section.ce:para: An experimental analysis of the BP was conducted. Because testing complete BPs requires large loads and poses safety risks, initial testing was performed on a single module. Each module had individual airflow inlets and outlets, mimicking the actual configuration and ensuring reliable preliminary analysis. A simple setup was designed, consisting of a resistive load bank and a BMS to control the contactors. The single-module setup is shown in Fig. 18 , with an enlarged section highlighting the thermistor positions.

The module was connected to the load bank through a contactor, allowing easy disconnection in emergencies. A fuse was included in the current flow path for added protection. The load bank featured six manually controlled resistors (four rated at 20 A and two rated at 10 A), which were adjusted according to the drive cycle profile used in simulations. A continuous current of 50 A and a pulse current of 90 A with a 15% duty cycle were applied to the module for 20 min. During this time, the BMS monitored and recorded the cells’ temperatures, as well as the voltage and current through the module. Because no forced convection occurred in this scenario, a significant temperature increase was observed. To simulate the modeled conditions, a BP was installed in the vehicle and tested under the FS drive cycle profile. The vehicle was driven on a track for 20 min, covering 11 km, at an ambient air temperature of 32 °C. During the test, a LUG-type thermistor was bolted directly to the cell via a bus bar to record the BP’s temperature. These recorded data were then analyzed and compared with the simulation results to complete the analysis.

The average deviation of the results obtained for temperature (Fig. 19 ) was 4.256% from the simulated results; hence, the model designed in MATLAB was validated with the practically obtained data. Maximum deviations of 4.41%, 5.43%, 3.85%, 3.42%, and 4.17% were observed at 250, 500, 750, 1,000 and 1,200 s, respectively, as shown in Fig. 19.

The restrictions of BTMS in Formula Bharat electric cars are as follows:
•
The addition of air-conditioning systems increases the weight of the car, which is disadvantageous in a racing situation when every kilogram is counted. Additional components such as fans may affect the vehicle’s total weight and balance.
•
In an energy-constrained race such as Formula Bharat, balancing the power allocation to the BTMS and drivetrain is critical.
•
Although passive cooling technologies, such as air cooling, are simpler, they may not offer the rapid reaction required during peak demand scenarios. Active systems (such as liquid cooling) are quicker but more complicated, heavy, and expensive.
•
The BTMS comprises various mechanical and electrical components. If any of these components fail, the battery may quickly overheat, causing a performance reduction or safety hazards during a race.

The research objectives were successfully achieved, providing key insights into the temperature management of LIBPs for student EVs. The following conclusions were drawn:
•
The design and implementation of a temperature management system effectively regulated battery temperatures within the targeted range of 40–50 °C. Maintaining this range is essential for ensuring optimal battery performance, longevity, and efficiency.
•
The CFD analysis indicated that the best cooling performance of the module occurred in Case V, where an inlet air velocity of 17 m·s−¹ was utilized, leading to a temperature range of 40−50 °C.
•
A model for heat generation was successfully developed using the MATLAB software. Experimental tests were performed to confirm the accuracy of the heat generation models and simulations. The simulated findings indicated that the average variance in the temperature data was 4.256%. The strong correlation between the experimental and predicted values demonstrates the reliability of the models used in this study.
The findings of this research offer valuable insights and practical guidelines for the thermal management of LIBPs in FSEVs, enhancing their reliability, safety, and lifespan.

Sagar Wankhede: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ajay D. Pingale: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Atharva Kale: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.
